Title: Split logging web UI: proxy + llama-server tail

Problem statement
-----------------
The current `/logs` web UI tails only the proxy log. Operators and developers also need live access to the llama-server stdout/stderr log (captured to `llama-server.log`) while troubleshooting model loads and runtime errors. We will split the view into two vertical panes: one showing the existing proxy log tail and one that tails the llama-server log.

Users
-----
- Support engineers and developers debugging model start/load failures and runtime errors. Example story: "As an on-call engineer, I want to see both proxy and llama-server logs side-by-side so I can correlate a failing request in the proxy with the llama-server startup messages."  
- Local operators running the proxy+llama locally using `proxyctl`/scripts who need an in-browser tail for convenience.

Success criteria
----------------
- The `/logs` UI provides two vertically split panes: left pane tails `proxy.log` (existing behavior), right pane tails `llama-server.log` with the same controls (lines, connect/disconnect, clear, download, autoscroll).  
- The llama-server pane streams initial N lines then appends new lines via SSE or a combined server stream; switching panes or disconnecting does not crash the server.  
- Implement server-side support so both sources can be served reliably in development and production (respecting configured `log_dir` and `/var/log/llama-proxy`), including handling rotated/missing files gracefully.  
- UI clearly labels each pane and the downloaded file names (e.g., `proxy.log`, `llama-server.log`).  
- No regression to existing proxy log tailing behavior and automated tests (if present) keep passing.

Constraints
-----------
- The proxy process already implements `/logs/tail` SSE for `proxy.log`; prefer lightweight changes that reuse existing SSE infrastructure.  
- In production the logs may be under `/var/log/llama-proxy` and may require read permissions; do not assume root. The server currently falls back to `./logs`.  
- Avoid large architectural changes (no full websocket rework) unless necessary.  
- WL (worklog) operations could not be completed in this environment due to a local worklog DB migration mismatch; this brief is saved as a draft until WL is available.

Existing state
--------------
- Server: `server.py` implements `/logs` (HTML UI) and `/logs/tail` SSE streaming for `proxy.log`. The UI is a single-pane tail with controls and an injected `window.__INITIAL_STATS` snapshot. (see `server.py` /logs and /logs/tail handlers).  
- Logs: README documents `proxy.log` and `llama-server.log` usage and rotation; `start_llama.sh` and `server.start_llama_server()` manage `llama-server.log` rotation.  
- `proxyctl` has a `logs` command to tail the proxy log locally.  

Desired change
--------------
- Add a second SSE source for `llama-server.log` (options below) and update `/logs` UI to show two vertically split panes with identical controls.  
- Server-side options (implementor choice; recommended server-side merge if feasible):  
  - Option A (recommended by user): Server-side merges proxy + llama logs into a single SSE stream with labeled messages and the UI renders two panes by subscribing to appropriate sources (or the merged stream can include labeled events for both).  
  - Option B: Add a dedicated endpoint `/logs/llama/tail` that mirrors `/logs/tail` but for `llama-server.log`.  
  - Option C: Extend `/logs/tail` with a `?source=` param (e.g. `?source=llama`) and reuse the same handler for both sources.  

Related work (automated report)
-------------------------------
- `server.py` — /logs and /logs/tail handlers: implements existing proxy UI and SSE tail logic; source of most UI and SSE behavior to reuse. (file: `server.py`, lines around `/logs` and `/logs/tail`)  
- `README.md` — documents logs, where `llama-server.log` is kept and rotated; useful for acceptance criteria and commands for operators. (file: `README.md` lines referencing `llama-server.log` and tails)  
- `proxyctl` — CLI `logs` command that tails proxy logs; useful reference for file locations and dev UX. (file: `proxyctl`)  
- `config.yaml` — contains log-related config values (retention, directories). (file: `config.yaml`)  

Implementation notes (suggested)
------------------------------
- Reuse `read_last_n()` helper (in `/logs/tail`) for initial block and the SSE event generator pattern for streaming new lines.  
- Mirror the proxy SSE message shape: initial block as `{'initial': <text>}`, appended messages as `{'line': <text>}`, and counts/tokens events unchanged. For merged streams add a `source` key, e.g., `{'source':'llama','line': '...'} ` or `{'source':'proxy','line':'...'}` when merging server-side.  
- Ensure file-rotation handling is mirrored (detect smaller file size -> reset read position).  
- Downloads should use the original file name when saving (server UI already sets `proxy.log` as download filename).  

Acceptance criteria (developer-concrete)
-------------------------------------
1. Two-pane UI: visiting `/logs` shows two labeled vertical panes: `Proxy log` and `Llama-server log`.  
2. SSE endpoints: either a merged SSE stream containing a `source` field, or a separate SSE endpoint `/logs/llama/tail` that returns identical message shapes as `/logs/tail`.  
3. Both panes support: initial N lines, append streaming, autoscroll, clear, download (filename matches source), connect/disconnect.  
4. Server-side respects configured `log_dir` and gracefully handles missing/rotated files (send `{info: 'log_rotated_or_removed'}` or similar).  
5. Tests: smoke test that `/logs` returns 200 and that `/logs/tail?lines=1` returns initial payload when a log file exists (add a small unit/integration test or a simple assertion if present test harness exists).  

Appendix — Clarifying questions and answers
-------------------------------------------
- Q: "How should the llama-server log be sourced and exposed to the web UI?" — Answer (user): "Proxy server to combine both logs into a single stream". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.  
- Q: "Should the llama-server pane have the same controls as the proxy pane (lines, connect/disconnect, clear, download, autoscroll)?" — Answer (user): "Yes — same controls (recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.  
- Q: "Worklog (wl) DB migration: Worklog commands failed due to a DB schema mismatch. How should I proceed?" — Answer (user): "Proceed now without updating wl (draft intake file only) (recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.  

Potentially related docs
------------------------
- server.py - Implements `/logs`, `/logs/tail`, SSE handling and UI HTML.  
- README.md - Describes `proxy.log` and `llama-server.log` locations and tail commands.  
- proxyctl - CLI helper with `logs` command; references log directory.  
- config.yaml - Log directory and retention settings.

Potentially related work items
-----------------------------
- No matching work items found in local Worklog (wl search returned no results). (Worklog DB: pending migrations prevented creating/updating work items in this run.)

Notes about worklog (WL) operations
----------------------------------
- Attempts to create/update the work item via `wl` failed due to a local Worklog DB schema mismatch (pending migrations). The user instructed to proceed with draft-only intake. Once WL is fixed (run `wl doctor upgrade --confirm` or otherwise repair), apply this file as the work item description with `wl create` or `wl update --description-file` and set stage/status as appropriate.

# Intake Brief: Proxy Blocks When Responding to a Request

## Problem Statement
The proxy blocks when the `/llama/local/status` endpoint is called after an OpenAI API request completes. The first status check succeeds; subsequent calls hang indefinitely. This suggests blocking in httpx client management when connecting to a busy llama-server.

## Users
- Developers and operators using the proxy to route OpenAI-compatible requests to local llama-server
- Users monitoring model status via the `/llama/local/status` endpoint or web UI

Example user story: "As an operator, I need to query the llama-server status at any time, even when the server is actively processing a request, so I can monitor system health without causing disruptions."

## Success Criteria
1. The `/llama/local/status` endpoint returns within 5 seconds even when llama-server is busy processing requests
2. Multiple concurrent status requests do not block each other
3. The proxy remains responsive during streaming and non-streaming local requests
4. No connection exhaustion or deadlocks occur under concurrent load
5. Existing tests pass and new tests verify non-blocking behavior

## Constraints
- The llama-server is a separate process (possibly single-threaded) that handles model inference
- The proxy uses FastAPI with asyncio for async operations
- Status queries must not interfere with ongoing requests
- The fix should not require architectural changes to FastAPI or the existing async model

## Risks & Assumptions
- **Assumption**: The blocking is caused by httpx client behavior (connection limits, no pooling), not a fundamental deadlock in the async code.
- **Assumption**: The llama-server can respond to status queries even when busy processing a request (though it may be slower).
- **Risk**: Scope creep - fixing httpx client configuration may reveal other blocking issues that could expand the fix scope. Mitigation: Record additional findings as new linked work items rather than expanding this bug fix.
- **Risk**: The fix may require changing how httpx clients are created/shared, which could impact other parts of the proxy. Mitigation: Test thoroughly and ensure backward compatibility.
- **Failure mode**: If the fix requires longer timeouts or more connections, it may mask underlying performance issues in llama-server.

## Existing State
- `query_llama_status()` creates a new `httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0)` per call with no connection pooling
- Streaming handlers (`proxy_to_local`) create `httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=None)` per request
- `_periodic_broadcast_loop` calls `query_llama_status()` every 1 second for SSE broadcasts
- No httpx connection limits configured; default limits may cause exhaustion under concurrent load

## Desired Change
1. Configure httpx.AsyncClient with appropriate connection limits and reuse clients via pooling
2. Ensure all httpx operations have timeouts to prevent indefinite blocking
3. Add concurrent status request handling that doesn't block the proxy
4. Verify fix with tests that simulate concurrent streaming requests and status queries

## Related Work
- `proxy/server.py` — Main proxy implementation. Key sections: `query_llama_status()` (line 336) creates httpx.AsyncClient for status checks; streaming handlers (line 1230-1298) proxy to llama-server; SSE endpoints `/events` (line 3087) and `/logs/tail` broadcast status updates; `_periodic_broadcast_loop` (line 439) calls status queries every 1 second.
- `tests/test_llama_local_status.py` — Existing tests for the status endpoint covering server running/not running and model switch states.
- `LP-0MNTCBP4900652XO` — "Implement non-blocking model load and immediate 503 response" (in-progress, high priority). Related non-blocking work that addresses similar concurrency issues in model loading. Not a duplicate - this bug is specifically about status endpoint blocking, not model loading blocking.

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions and Answers
- Q: "Is the llama-server single-threaded or can it handle concurrent HTTP requests?" — Answer (agent inference): Unknown from code. The bug description suggests the llama-server may block new connections while processing requests. Need to verify with testing. Source: code review of `query_llama_status()` and streaming handlers.
- Q: "Does the issue occur with both streaming and non-streaming requests?" — Answer (agent inference): The bug report mentions "OpenAI request" which typically uses streaming. The blocking may be more apparent with streaming requests as they hold connections longer. Source: bug description.
- Q: "What is the expected behavior when llama-server is busy?" — Answer (agent inference): The status endpoint should return quickly with available information rather than blocking. Source: code analysis of `_periodic_broadcast_loop` which calls `query_llama_status()` every 1 second.

# Investigate /logs endpoint hang during active streaming request

## Problem Statement
Requests to /logs hang indefinitely when an LLM streaming request is in progress. This is a separate issue from the /llama/local/status blocking issue (LP-0MNWEZMUX009XL9B) which was about httpx connection pooling.

## Root Cause Found
In proxy/server.py line 3728, view_logs() calls router_list_models() when router_mode is enabled. router_list_models() (line 719) creates a new httpx.AsyncClient and makes a blocking request to /models on llama-server with a 10 second timeout. If llama-server is busy with a streaming request, this blocks until the streaming completes (or timeout).

Additionally, the HTML template string replacement at line 3737 performs multiple string replacements on a very large HTML document which could also contribute to slow response times.

## Success Criteria
1. Fix the issue so /logs responds within reasonable time during active streaming
2. Add tests to verify /logs works during active streaming

## Related Work
- LP-0MNWEZMUX009XL9B: Fixed /llama/local/status blocking with connection pooling

Headline
Set `gemma4` as the system-wide default model while keeping GPT-oss available as a selectable option; update runtime, config, aliases, tests, and docs so the change is visible and reversible.

Problem statement
Set Gemma 4 (`gemma4`) as the system-wide default model while preserving GPT-oss as a selectable option; update runtime, configuration, aliases, tests, and docs so the system, operators, and developers observe the new default behavior.

Users
- Platform operators and SREs: need a predictable default model that is maintained in configuration and logs.
- Developers and feature owners (Casey alias): expect the default model to respond to the previously used alias and behave as documented.

Success criteria
- The application loads `gemma4` as the default model from config and at runtime without errors (verify by startup logs and status endpoint showing `gemma4` as active when default is used).
- The default pointer in configuration is updated to `gemma4` and examples/docs clearly show GPT-oss remains available as a selectable option.
- The `Casey` alias resolves to `gemma4` at runtime and requests using the `Casey` alias are served by `gemma4` (verify with integration test and runtime logs).
- Automated tests (unit and integration) are added/updated to validate default model selection and alias resolution; tests pass in CI and locally.
- All related documentation (README, code comments, docs site) is updated to reflect the new default and preserved GPT-oss option.

Constraints

Constraints
 - Identifier to use: `gemma4` (confirmed).
 - Scope: global immediate replacement of the default pointer (change runtime, config, and docs everywhere unless blocked by other work items).
 - Rollback approach: manual rollback acceptable (no dedicated rollback flag required per current decision).

 - Important: Do not remove GPT-oss or its configuration entries; GPT-oss must remain available as a selectable option in config and at runtime.

Existing state
- Worklog item: Add Gemma 4 as default in place of GPT-oss (LP-0MNXARX6A0045GJO) exists and is currently in stage `idea` and status `in-progress`.
- Repo evidence: configuration files and server code reference `default_model` and show examples using `qwen3`, `gpt120`, or other ids. Notable files:
  - `proxy/config.yaml` contains a `default_model` key (current value: "gpt120").
  - `proxy/README.md` documents `default_model: "qwen3"` as an example.
  - `proxy/server.py` contains runtime logic that loads and preloads a configured `default_model`.

Desired change
- Update configuration defaults and runtime references so `gemma4` is the canonical default model id (update the default pointer only).
- Ensure alias mapping includes `Casey` -> `gemma4`.
- Update tests and add integration/unit tests asserting default selection and alias resolution.
- Update README and any other docs that recommend or show the previous default; show GPT-oss as an available option in examples.

- Ensure GPT-oss remains present in configuration examples and selectable at runtime (do not delete existing GPT entries; update only the default pointer).

Risks & assumptions
- Risk: Default model change may impact downstream behaviour (latency, cost, compatibility). Mitigation: smoke-test critical flows and monitor latency and error rates after rollout.
- Risk: Alias conflicts with existing alias rules. Mitigation: run alias resolution tests and verify `Casey` maps uniquely to `gemma4`.
- Assumption: `gemma4` is already available to the runtime environment and loadable by the llama-server; if not, a small onboarding task is required.
- Scope-creep mitigation: record any additional requested changes (e.g., feature flags, staged rollout, migration of model assets) as separate linked work items rather than expanding this item.

Related work
- `LP-0MNXARX6A0045GJO` Add Gemma 4 as default in place of GPT-oss — primary work item (this item).
- `proxy/config.yaml` — contains `default_model` setting; will be updated as part of this change.
- `proxy/server.py` — runtime logic that loads `default_model`; requires code edits to preload or alias mapping.
- `README.md` — docs references to `default_model` examples to update.

- Potentially related work items discovered in repo search:
  - `LP-0MNA7FYH4001ZUR4` Router-mode llama-server integration — mentions default model ids and router preload behaviour.
  - `LP-0MNVM4L0C0033FS2` Provide REST status endpoint for llama.cpp server — returns currently loaded model; relevant for visibility/monitoring of default.
  - `LP-0MN8LU54M00774GG` Routing & alias behaviour for embeddings — related alias resolution concerns.


Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers

- Q: "Which model identifier should be set as the default in code/config?"
  — Answer (user): "gemma4 (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "What scope should this change have?"
  — Answer (user): "Global immediate replacement (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "What level of testing and rollback support should be added?"
  — Answer (user): "Manual rollback acceptable". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

Research & evidence (agent):
- Found `proxy/config.yaml` with `default_model: "gpt120"`. (file: `proxy/config.yaml`)
- Found `proxy/README.md` referencing `default_model: "qwen3"`. (file: `proxy/README.md`)
- Found logic in `proxy/server.py` that reads `default_model` and attempts to preload/ensure the model is loaded at startup. (file: `proxy/server.py`)

Notes on idempotence
- This draft includes the three clarifying Q/A pairs received during intake; re-running the intake should update these entries rather than duplicating them.

data.description:
Headline
--------
Add router-only unit tests that verify deterministic model load/unload transitions (embed + primary slot), ensure embedding requests do not trigger primary switches, and validate stable error/unload states.

Problem statement
-----------------
The repository lacks a focused, router-only unit test suite that proves deterministic model load and unload transitions for the embeddings slot + primary model. Without these tests, race conditions and non-deterministic behaviour during concurrent or error-prone model lifecycle events may regress co-residency and router-mode features.

Users
-----
- Engine developers and maintainers working on proxy/server.py lifecycle code.
- CI and QA engineers who rely on unit tests to catch regressions during refactors.
- Operators who need confidence that embedding requests will not unexpectedly switch the primary model.

Example user stories
- As an engineer, I can run unit tests that assert load state transitions for the embed alias are deterministic in router-mode.
- As an integration tester, I can run a lightweight stub-based test that simulates a llama-server stub to verify the proxy's state machine under a realistic load.
- As an operator, I expect embedding requests not to cause a primary model switch when co-residency is enabled; tests should assert this behaviour.

Success criteria
----------------
- New unit tests live at proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py and cover: deterministic state transitions for loading (requested → loading → loaded), that embedding requests do not trigger primary switches when co-residency is enabled, unload/reload transitions, and failed load handling leaving a stable error state.
- At least one lightweight stub-based integration-style test is present (in the same test file or proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_integration.py) that runs against a minimal HTTP stub of llama-server to validate the state machine end-to-end.
- Tests are deterministic and CI-friendly (mocked HTTP for unit tests; the stub test is allowed but marked as slow if necessary). All added tests pass locally and in CI on the default test runner.
- All related documentation (test file headers, proxy/README.md where tests are referenced, and any code comments) is updated to reflect the new tests.
- Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.

Constraints
-----------
- Scope: Router-mode only; do not modify non-router mode behavior in this task.
- Test design: Prefer pure unit tests with mocks for the state machine and HTTP responses; include a single stub-based integration-style test for realism (mix approach selected).
- Keep tests lightweight and deterministic; avoid long sleep-based waits where possible.

Existing state
--------------
- proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py contains router-mode unit tests and helper patterns that the new tests should follow and extend.
- proxy/server.py implements lifecycle helpers: ensure_model_loaded, schedule_background_load, router_load_model, router_wait_for_model, and global runtime state (current_model, background_loads). These are the implementation targets.
- Related parent/ancestor work exists: LP-0MNA7G07U0020NDC (Model lifecycle: embed + primary slot) and LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN (Co-residency runtime support). A co-residency spike (LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX) produced memory guidance and related notes.

Desired change
--------------
- Add a new test file: proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py containing unit tests that:
  - Use pytest + pytest-asyncio and monkeypatch to stub start/wait/router functions and to simulate HTTP responses for llama-server endpoints when required.
  - Implement tests for the scenarios selected: (1) deterministic load state transitions; (2) embedding requests do not trigger primary model switches; (4) unload/reload deterministic behaviour; (5) failed load leaves stable error state.
- Add a single stub-based integration-style test (in the same test file or a sibling file) that runs a minimal HTTP stub server to emulate llama-server lifecycle endpoints and verifies end-to-end behaviour for one or two scenarios.
- Update proxy/README.md (tests section) with instructions to run the new router-only tests and note any slow/stub tests.

Related work (automated report)
-------------------------------
- LP-0MNA7G07U0020NDC — Model lifecycle: embed + primary slot (parent). Describes the broader feature, acceptance criteria, and references existing tests. This child task implements the deterministic unit test coverage requested by the parent. (LP-0MNA7G07U0020NDC)
- LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN — Co-residency runtime support (ancestor). Contains design notes, constraints, and requested tests for router-mode lifecycle; relevant for test scenarios and risk considerations. (LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN)
- LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX — Co-residency spike: llama-server API & memory (completed). Provides runtime observations and artifacts for choosing a model pair and measuring memory; useful for deciding which behaviors to stub in integration-style tests. (LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX)
- LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC — Add an embeddings specific model (completed). Documents aliasing and examples used in existing tests and configs; useful for sample model names and expected resolution semantics. (LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC)
- proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py — Existing unit tests and patterns to extend. Demonstrates usage of monkeypatch and background loader markers; good examples to follow. (proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py)
- proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py — Integration test patterns for embeddings; useful for deciding stub behaviour and expectations for embeddings responses. (proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py)

Risks & assumptions
-------------------
- Risk: Tests may rely on internal global state in proxy/server.py which can lead to brittle tests if implementation changes. Mitigation: reset module-level state at test setup and use monkeypatch to replace external dependencies.
- Risk: Slow or flaky tests if integration stub uses sleep-based timing. Mitigation: prefer mocked unit tests and keep stub test minimal and timeboxed; mark slow tests to be excluded by default in fast CI runs.
- Risk: Scope creep — adding broader integration tests or modifying runtime logic. Mitigation: restrict this work item to router-only unit tests and one minimal stub test; any additional tests become separate follow-ups.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
--------------------------------------
- Q: "Where should the new unit tests live? (pick one)
- A: Extend proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py (add router-only cases)
- B: New file proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py (keeps router-only tests separate)
- C: Other — please give path." — Answer (user): "B" (user). Source: interactive reply. Final: New file `proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py`.

- Q: "Which minimal scenarios must be covered by the deterministic lifecycle tests? (pick one or more)
- 1: Load embed model (router-only) and assert state transitions: requested → loading → loaded
- 2: Ensure embedding request does NOT trigger primary model switch when co-residency enabled
- 3: Concurrent requests: simultaneous embed + primary requests produce deterministic ordering (no race/flip-flop)
- 4: Unload/reload transitions: ensure unload leads to expected state and subsequent loads are deterministic
- 5: Error handling: failed load yields stable error state and does not leave partial/mixed state" — Answer (user): "items 1, 2, 4 and 5" (user). Source: interactive reply. Final: Include scenarios 1, 2, 4, 5.

- Q: "Test style: should these be pure unit tests that mock llama-server / HTTP calls, or should we run against a lightweight stub process?
- A: Pure unit tests with pytest + pytest-asyncio, mocking HTTP (fast, CI-friendly) — recommended
- B: Lightweight stub llama-server (more realistic, slower)
- C: Mix: unit tests for state machines + one integration-style test with stub" — Answer (user): "C" (user). Source: interactive reply. Final: Mix approach; prefer unit tests + one stub-based integration-style test.

Appendix: End.

data.description:
Problem statement:
Implement session-based incremental ingestion in our llama-server client/proxy integration so that the server reuses a per-session KV cache and ingests only new tokens, instead of re-ingesting the full conversation on every request. This reduces CPU and latency for multi-turn conversations.

Users:
- Proxy users and clients making multi-turn requests to llama-server (internal services, Python clients, curl-based tests).
- Example user story: "As a client developer, I want the proxy to reuse a session so that subsequent messages in a conversation are fast and use less CPU."

Success criteria:
- The proxy/client can create and reuse a `session_id` so llama-server persists KV cache across requests.
- Subsequent requests for the same session send only new messages/tokens; logs show llama-server ingesting only the new tokens.
- A Python client example and a curl example demonstrate session creation, reuse, and observable reduction in ingestion time.
- Performance validation: example benchmark shows measurable reduction (e.g., >= 50% CPU time or ingestion time) vs full-history re-ingestion for multi-turn scenarios.
- All related documentation is updated; full project test suite passes.

Constraints:
- Editing earlier messages invalidates the KV cache; callers must re-ingest full history when edits occur.
- Context window and KV cache eviction limits imposed by llama-server must be respected.
- Sessions are ephemeral (in-memory) unless persisted by llama-server; cross-restart durability is not required for initial implementation.

Existing state:
- Current client/proxy re-sends full conversation on each request; a draft work item exists describing session-based incremental ingestion (LP-0MO6DJP0P006WCHA).
- Repo contains llama-server docs (LLAMA_README.md), router docs (docs/llama-router.md), and proxy implementation files (proxy/proxy/server.py) that currently interact with llama-server.

Desired change:
- Update proxy/client to generate/manage `session_id` values and forward only incremental messages to llama-server when a session is reused.
- Provide example clients (Python, curl), a minimal proxy helper, and documentation.
- Add tests/benchmarks validating reduced ingestion.

Risks & assumptions:
- Risk: Editing earlier messages requires full re-ingestion and may invalidate per-session KV caches. Mitigation: Document this limitation and provide client guidance; record follow-up work if edit-preserve becomes required.
- Risk: Sessions held in memory may increase memory usage and require eviction policies. Mitigation: Define session lifetime and eviction/rotation policy; log metrics to observe memory usage.
- Risk: Misuse (clients accidentally resending full history) could negate benefits. Mitigation: Proxy should detect and strip duplicate history when safe, and document recommended client patterns.
- Assumption: llama-server supports per-session KV caches keyed by `session_id` (confirmed by existing work item description). Source: LP-0MO6DJP0P006WCHA description.
- Scope-scope creep mitigation: Any significant follow-ups (session persistence across restarts, cross-node session sharing, or edit-preserve features) will be recorded as separate work items linked to this parent.

Related work (automated report)
- LLAMA_README.md: Provides high-level guidance for running llama-server and how the proxy interacts with it. Relevant for integration and deployment notes.
- docs/llama-router.md: Documents router-mode operation for llama-server when hosting multiple models; relevant if sessions must be routed or sharded across router children.
- proxy/README.md: Describes how the proxy starts/manages llama-server and contains architecture context for how client requests are forwarded; useful for implementation details and examples.
- proxy/proxy/server.py: Primary proxy implementation that currently forwards requests to llama-server and manages SSE/status broadcasts. This is the main integration point for session handling and where `session_id` logic should be implemented.
- Work item LP-0MNWEZMUX009XL9B ("Proxy blocks when responding to a request"): Addresses connection pooling and non-blocking httpx client usage when talking to llama-server. Relevant because session-based incremental ingestion will interact with the same client plumbing and should reuse existing shared client and timeout/limits practices.
- Work item LP-0MNWV7I8M002IWL4 ("Fix repeated 404s querying llama-server status"): Describes resilient endpoint discovery and caching for llama-server status endpoints; useful when implementing query/fallback logic for session metadata or capabilities.
- Work item LP-0MN5AW8DE1KJVAKX ("Add stats to the main page for the currently loaded model"): Implements SSE broadcasting of model metadata (n_ctx, KV cache counts) and related tests; relevant for validation and telemetry during session reuse testing.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
- No clarifying questions were required; agent proceeded using the existing work item content as authoritative. (Answer source: work item LP-0MO6DJP0P006WCHA description.)

data.description:
## Summary
Implement hybrid session_id generation (client-supplied or proxy-generated) with 3-hour TTL and in-memory session registry.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Proxy accepts X-Session-Id header from client; if absent, proxy generates opaque UUID v4 session_id
- Proxy returns X-Session-Id header in response for all requests (echo client-provided or return generated)
- Sessions expire after 3 hours of inactivity; expired sessions are evicted from memory
- Session registry tracks: session_id, created_at, last_activity_at, message_count
- Unit tests cover generation, acceptance, TTL expiry, and eviction logic
- Negative case: When session_id is invalid/expired, proxy falls back to full history ingestion

## Minimal Implementation
- Add SessionManager class in proxy/proxy/server.py with dict-based in-memory storage
- Add session_id extraction from headers and generation fallback
- Add background cleanup task for expired sessions (simple periodic scan)
- Add response header injection for session_id
- Unit tests in proxy/tests/test_session_manager.py

## Dependencies
None (foundational feature)

## Deliverables
- Code: SessionManager class, header handling in request/response path
- Tests: test_session_manager.py (unit tests)
- Docs: Update proxy/README.md with session_id header documentation

## Summary
Modify proxy to forward only new messages to llama-server when session_id is reused, leveraging llama-server's per-session KV cache.

## Acceptance Criteria
- When session_id is reused, proxy sends only new messages (not full conversation history) to llama-server
- llama-server logs show ingestion of only new tokens (verified via log parsing in tests)
- Proxy tracks per-session message history to determine new messages
- Full history is sent when session_id is new or when client indicates edit (no special edit handling in proxy)
- Integration test validates reduced payload size on subsequent requests
- Negative case: Benchmark fails gracefully if llama-server unavailable

## Minimal Implementation
- Extend SessionManager to store per-session message list (role, content tuples)
- Modify chat completion request handler to compare incoming messages vs stored history
- Compute delta (new messages only) and forward delta to llama-server
- Update session history after successful llama-server response
- Integration test in proxy/tests/test_incremental_ingestion.py

## Dependencies
LP-0MO6EWJ5K0088CBS (Session ID generation and management core)

## Deliverables
- Code: Delta computation logic, history tracking in SessionManager
- Tests: Integration test validating incremental vs full payload
- Docs: Add example to proxy/README.md showing incremental request pattern

-  starts  via  (start_llama_server). If the distrobox/podman runtime or user runtime dir is unavailable, the llama-server process can fail immediately; wait_for_llama_server logs the process exit code and returns failure.
- Systemd service  sets  and . These depend on a logged-in user session and may not exist right after boot, causing distrobox/podman to fail to start containers.
- I could not find  in the logs directory (expected at ), indicating the llama-server did not produce output or log rotation removed it;  and  exist and are empty/small.

Hypothesis (most likely): On reboot the systemd service starts before a user session is available (or in an environment where  isn't present), so distrobox version: 1.8.2.2

Supported models: GPT120, Qwen3, Qwen2.5 directly without distrobox for systemd-managed startup. Add  (or the appropriate container socket) and  /  to ensure runtime availability.

data.author: OpenCode
data.comment:
Implemented retry + stderr/stdout capture fallback in  and updated  to depend on  and .

Changes:
- : start_llama_server now: tries distrobox when present, falls back to direct script, captures immediate child output, streams long-running stdout to , retries with backoff, and broadcasts a detailed error message when all attempts fail.
- : added , ,  and removed hardcoded user runtime env lines.

Next: I can (1) add unit tests for the fallback logic and a small integration/smoke test that asserts the retry behavior, (2) update README troubleshooting with the new unit file recommendations, and (3) create a PR. Which of these should I do next? (Recommended: 2 then 3)

data.author: OpenCode
data.comment: Removed repo-distributed systemd unit files and updated docs/install scripts to remove installation instructions. Files changed: proxy/README.md, proxy/install.sh, proxy/HOST_INSTALL.md, proxy/setup-linger-uidmap.sh. See commit fef17e9.

data.author: OpenCode
data.comment: Added safe host cleanup script to remove or mask an installed llama-proxy systemd unit: proxy/scripts/remove-host-llama-proxy-service.sh. See commit d942bf3.

data.author: OpenCode
data.comment: Added diagnostic script to help determine what starts the proxy after reboot: proxy/scripts/diagnose-llama-proxy-startup.sh. See commit a59cf78. Run it as root: sudo bash proxy/scripts/diagnose-llama-proxy-startup.sh and paste the output.

data.author: OpenCode
data.comment: Added helper to remove a user-level llama-proxy unit: proxy/scripts/remove-user-llama-proxy-service.sh. Run as root: sudo ./proxy/scripts/remove-user-llama-proxy-service.sh rgardler. See commit 8671523.

data.author: OpenCode
data.comment:
Spike investigation complete. Key findings:

**RECOMMENDATION: Co-load NOT supported**

llama-server is architected as a single-model-per-process server. It does NOT support loading multiple independent models concurrently.

**Memory Analysis:**
- mxbai-embed-large-v1: ~1 GB
- gpt-oss-120b: ~69 GB  
- Total required: ~70 GB (available: 122 GB ✓)
- Memory is NOT the limiting factor

**API Capabilities:**
- Endpoints: /health, /props, /v1/models, /v1/embeddings, /v1/chat/completions
- No dynamic model loading API exists
- No hot-swap capability
- Process restart required for model changes

**Recommended Next Steps (Option A):**
Run separate llama-server instances:
- Port 8080: gpt-oss-120b (chat/completions)
- Port 8081: mxbai-embed-large-v1 (embeddings)

This provides:
- Clean separation of concerns
- No model switching latency
- Embeddings always available
- Standard llama-server usage pattern

Full report saved to: .opencode/tmp/co-residency-spike-results.md

**Estimated effort for implementation:** 2-3 days (Medium)

data.author: OpenCode
data.comment:
**CORRECTION:** Router mode discovery changes recommendation

I initially missed that llama-server includes **router mode** - a native multi-model hosting capability.

**Router Mode Capabilities:**
- Hosts multiple models in isolated child processes
- Automatic LRU memory management (--models-max)
- Management API: GET /models, POST /models/load, POST /models/unload
- Automatic request routing based on model name/alias

**UPDATED RECOMMENDATION: Implement router mode**

Key CLI options:


**Implementation approach:**
1. Create models.ini preset with both model configurations
2. Start llama-server without --model (enters router mode)
3. Simplify proxy - remove manual switching logic
4. Router handles all model lifecycle automatically

**Benefits:**
- Native multi-model support
- Automatic memory management
- Minimal proxy code changes
- Clean child process isolation

Full corrected report: .opencode/tmp/co-residency-spike-results.md

Apologies for the initial oversight. Router mode is exactly what's needed for co-residency.

data.author: OpenCode
data.comment:
Configuration Update: Set --models-max to 4

Based on operator guidance, router mode should be configured with:
--models-max 4 (not 2)

This allows:
- Embeddings model (mxbai-embed-large-v1)
- Primary model (gpt-oss-120b)
- 2 additional slots for future models or hot-swapping

Benefits of --models-max 4:
- Flexibility to add more models without config changes
- Headroom for model variants (different quantizations)
- Better LRU cache behavior with multiple slots
- System has 122 GB RAM available, can easily support 4 models

Updated in spike report: .opencode/tmp/co-residency-spike-results.md

data.author: @opencode
data.comment:
Root cause identified and fixed: The local httpx/ directory at the project root was shadowing the real httpx package (installed in .venv). This shim used synchronous requests.get()/requests.post() inside async functions, which completely blocked the asyncio event loop on every HTTP call to llama-server. This is why the proxy hung (and even Ctrl-C didn't work) when llama-server was busy.

Fix: Renamed httpx/ to test_httpx_shim/ so it no longer shadows the real httpx package. Updated conftest.py to stop replacing sys.modules['httpx']. Updated server.py to use real httpx.Timeout and httpx.Limits directly (no more hasattr guards). Updated wait_for_llama_server() and router_load_model() to use the shared _http_client. Updated test_llama_local_status.py to use httpx.ASGITransport (required by httpx 0.28.1). Added test_async_not_blocking.py with 3 tests that verify the event loop is not blocked during concurrent requests.

Files changed:
- httpx/ -> test_httpx_shim/ (renamed, no content changes)
- conftest.py (removed shim injection)
- proxy/server.py (simplified client creation, use shared client everywhere)
- tests/test_llama_local_status.py (use httpx.ASGITransport)
- tests/test_httpx_pooling.py (removed sys.path hack, cleaned up imports)
- tests/test_async_not_blocking.py (new test file)

All 24 tests pass.
